notes: Series data is fetched from APIs at build time, then converted into a static dashboard JSON file.
Daily and weekly series are resampled to month-start using the last observation in each month.
FOMC meeting metadata comes from data/fomc_events.csv so the dashboard can preserve full meeting history, including hold events.

layers.label: Policy Center
layers.description: The Fed decision itself and the policy tools closest to it.

layers.label: Immediate Market Reaction
layers.description: Markets that tend to reprice first after a Fed decision.

layers.label: Credit Transmission
layers.description: Borrowing costs and rate-sensitive sectors that transmit policy into the economy.

layers.label: Macro Outcomes
layers.description: Slower-moving labor, housing, and inflation outcomes.

lenses.label: Immediate Market Reaction
lenses.description: Prioritize yields, broad equity moves, volatility, and global spillovers.

lenses.label: Credit Transmission
lenses.description: Focus on bank lending conditions, mortgage rates, and rate-sensitive sectors.

lenses.label: Macro Outcomes
lenses.description: Track housing, jobs, and inflation as the downstream ripple arrives.

lenses.label: Full Cascade
lenses.description: View every layer together as one policy ripple.

series.label: Federal Funds Rate
series.description: The overnight interest rate banks charge each other. This is the policy rate closest to the Fed's decision.
series.example: Example: if the Fed tightens policy, the Fed Funds rate usually rises almost immediately.

series.label: Fed Funds Target Lower
series.description: The lower edge of the Fed's official target range for short-term rates.
series.example: Example: a target range of 5.25% to 5.50% would have 5.25% as the lower bound.

series.label: Fed Funds Target Upper
series.description: The upper edge of the Fed's official target range for short-term rates.
series.example: Example: when the Fed raises rates by 25 basis points, this upper bound usually moves up by 0.25%.

series.label: M2 Money Stock
series.description: A broad measure of money in the economy, including cash, checking, and savings balances.
series.example: Example: during aggressive stimulus periods, M2 often expands quickly as money enters the system.

series.label: 2-Year Treasury Yield
series.description: The interest rate investors demand to hold a 2-year U.S. Treasury bond.
series.example: Example: if markets expect more Fed hikes, the 2-year yield often jumps within days.

series.label: 10-Year Treasury Yield
series.description: The benchmark 10-year government bond yield that influences borrowing costs across the economy.
series.example: Example: mortgage and corporate borrowing costs often move with the 10-year yield.

series.label: 30-Year Treasury Yield
series.description: The yield on very long-term U.S. government debt.
series.example: Example: a rise here suggests investors want more compensation to lock money up for decades.

series.label: 10Y-2Y Yield Spread
series.description: The gap between the 10-year and 2-year Treasury yields, often used to read the shape of the yield curve.
series.example: Example: when the spread turns negative, people often say the yield curve is inverted.

series.label: S&P 500
series.description: A broad stock-market index tracking 500 large U.S. companies.
series.example: Example: a surprise rate hike can push the S&P 500 lower if investors expect slower growth.

series.label: Dow Jones Industrial Average
series.description: A stock-market index of 30 major U.S. companies.
series.example: Example: financial news often quotes the Dow when reacting to Fed announcements the same day.

series.label: CBOE Volatility Index
series.description: A market fear gauge based on expected stock volatility.
series.example: Example: when investors panic around a surprise decision, the VIX often spikes upward.

series.label: SPDR Gold Shares
series.description: An exchange-traded fund that tracks gold prices and acts as a common safe-haven proxy.
series.example: Example: if investors worry about inflation or instability, they may rotate into gold.

series.label: Bitcoin
series.description: A crypto asset that sometimes trades like a risk asset and sometimes like an inflation hedge.
series.example: Example: during tightening cycles, Bitcoin has often behaved more like a high-risk tech asset than digital gold.

series.label: Vanguard All-World ex-US
series.description: An ETF covering developed stock markets outside the United States.
series.example: Example: a stronger U.S. dollar after Fed tightening can pressure non-U.S. equity returns.

series.label: iShares Emerging Markets
series.description: An ETF tracking emerging-market stocks, which are often sensitive to U.S. rate moves.
series.example: Example: if U.S. yields rise, capital can flow out of emerging markets and weigh on EEM.

series.label: Baa Corporate Bond Yield
series.description: A borrowing-cost benchmark for lower investment-grade U.S. companies.
series.example: Example: if this yield rises, companies with weaker credit usually face more expensive financing.

series.label: Bank Prime Loan Rate
series.description: The base rate banks offer to their most creditworthy business customers.
series.example: Example: variable-rate business loans often reset when the prime rate changes.

series.label: 30-Year Mortgage Rate
series.description: The average rate on a standard 30-year fixed home mortgage.
series.example: Example: when mortgage rates rise from 3% to 7%, monthly home payments become much less affordable.

series.label: Financial Select Sector SPDR
series.description: An ETF representing major U.S. financial firms like banks and insurers.
series.example: Example: banks can benefit when higher rates widen the spread between what they earn and what they pay.

series.label: Real Estate Select Sector SPDR
series.description: An ETF representing real-estate companies and REITs.
series.example: Example: higher financing costs often hurt real-estate valuations and can drag XLRE down.

series.label: Technology Select Sector SPDR
series.description: An ETF representing large U.S. technology companies.
series.example: Example: tech valuations often weaken when discount rates rise because future earnings are worth less today.

series.label: Energy Select Sector SPDR
series.description: An ETF representing oil, gas, and energy companies.
series.example: Example: if tighter policy slows growth expectations, demand-sensitive energy stocks can soften.

series.label: Utilities Select Sector SPDR
series.description: An ETF representing utility companies that often carry a lot of debt and pay dividends.
series.example: Example: rising rates can hurt utilities because their borrowing gets more expensive and bonds become stronger competition.

series.label: Unemployment Rate
series.description: The share of the labor force that is actively looking for work but does not have a job.
series.example: Example: after prolonged tightening, unemployment may drift higher as hiring slows.

series.label: Nonfarm Payrolls
series.description: The total number of paid U.S. jobs outside the farm sector.
series.example: Example: if payroll growth cools after rate hikes, it suggests the labor market is losing momentum.

series.label: Consumer Price Index
series.description: A widely used inflation measure based on the prices households pay for goods and services.
series.example: Example: if CPI slows months after hikes, that suggests demand may be cooling.

series.label: PCE Price Index
series.description: The Fed's preferred inflation measure, based on personal consumption spending.
series.example: Example: policymakers often cite PCE when explaining whether inflation is moving back toward target.

series.label: Housing Starts
series.description: The number of new residential construction projects started in a month.
series.example: Example: if high rates cool the housing market, builders may start fewer new homes.
